metadata.summary: Use case modeling captures WHO interacts with a system, WHAT they do with it, and WHY — as a sequence of actor-system interactions that yields observable value. Each use case has a name, a primary actor, a main flow, alternative flows, and exception flows. Use-case-driven BA is contractual: the UC catalog becomes the apples-to-apples grid for vendor evaluation and the traceability anchor for regulated-industry audits.

content.title: Use Case Modeling
content.summary: Use case modeling captures WHO interacts with a system, WHAT they do with it, and WHY — as a sequence of actor-system interactions that yields observable value. Each use case has a name (Verb + Noun), a primary actor, a main flow (5-9 steps), at least one alternative flow, and at least one exception flow. The deliverable is a UC spec plus a UC diagram showing actor-system boundaries. Use-case-driven BA is contractual: the UC catalog becomes the apples-to-apples grid for vendor evaluation and the traceability anchor for regulated-industry audits.

why.p: Requirements written as feature lists leave developers without user context, testers without scenario coverage, and projects without a basis for impact analysis when scope changes. UCs anchor requirements at actor-goal level — a stable abstraction above UI screens and API endpoints — giving all parties a shared language that survives redesigns. Traceability from actor goal to step to commit to test to audit evidence is the primary value in regulated sectors (21 CFR Part 11, ISO 13485, IEC 62304, SOX).

list.item: Enterprise kickoffs requiring a UML UC diagram + UC specs as SoW deliverables (SAP, Salesforce, Dynamics rollouts, banking core upgrades)
RFP/RFI authoring — UC catalog is the vendor evaluation grid
RUP-style use-case-driven projects in regulated or government contexts where iterations are scoped to UC sets
Regulated industries where UCs anchor actor goal → step → commit → test → audit evidence
Legacy migration (mainframe, Oracle Forms) where reverse-engineering screens into UCs is the safe path to a greenfield spec
Outsourced delivery where step-level UC specs replace ambient context the offshore team lacks
Stakeholder alignment workshops bridging executive outcomes and engineering features
Lean/startup contexts — JTBD, Opportunity Solution Trees, or story mapping deliver faster discovery without SoW UC obligations
Data/analytics platforms — DFDs and dimensional models better expose the meaningful structure
Internal tools with a single actor and fewer than 10 transactions — a one-page acceptance-criteria sheet suffices
ML/LLM surfaces with probabilistic responses — UC main flows assume deterministic system responses and mislead
Event-sourced/reactive architectures — event storming better exposes the structure
Teams already running smoothly on user stories + acceptance criteria — layering UCs duplicates work

rule.statement: A use case must have all four structural elements: actor, use case goal, system boundary, and scenario paths.
rule.rationale: Without all four, the UC is either a feature description (missing actor goal) or an org chart (missing system boundary).

list.item: Actor — who or what interacts with the system (human, system, or time trigger).
Use Case — the specific goal the actor achieves through the system.
System — the boundary of what is being built or analyzed.
Scenario — a specific path through the use case (main, alternative, or exception).

rule.statement: Actor type must be one of {human, system, time}. Job titles are not valid actor names.
rule.rationale: Job titles collapse to org-chart labels and change; goal-bearing actors (Customer, PaymentGateway, NightlyBatch) survive system redesigns.

list.item: Primary actors — initiate use cases to achieve their own goals.
Secondary actors — support the system (databases, external services, schedulers).
External systems — other systems that the UC interacts with to complete a goal.

rule.statement: Use include for shared behavior that always executes; use extend for optional behavior that adds to a base UC at declared extension points.
rule.rationale: The directionality of extend is consistently inverted — extension points belong to the base UC; the extending UC references them. Inverting this breaks traceability.

list.item: Include — base UC always calls the included UC (e.g., Place Order includes Process Payment).
Extend — extending UC optionally adds behavior at a declared extension point (e.g., Apply Coupon extends Place Order).
Generalization — specialized UC inherits the structure of a parent UC (e.g., Pay with Credit Card generalizes Pay).

rule.statement: Every UC must pass all nine quality gates before it is baselined.
rule.rationale: Missing any gate — especially alternative flows and exception flows — produces happy-path-only specs that fail in production.

list.item: Use case name is Verb + Noun.
Clear primary actor identified with type in {human, system, time}.
Preconditions are verifiable (not aspirational).
Main flow has 5-9 steps; each step = one actor action + one system response.
At least one alternative flow documented.
At least one exception flow documented.
Postconditions defined for success and failure.
Business rules cited by ID.
Reviewed and signed off by stakeholders.

rule.statement: Lock the system boundary in writing before identifying actors. Every actor identification prompt must include that boundary paragraph verbatim.
rule.rationale: Without a written boundary, actors expand to include every person who touches any related system, producing an unmanageable diagram.

list.item: Who will use this system?
What external systems connect to this system?
Who maintains the system or receives its outputs?
What time-based or event-based triggers initiate system behavior?

rule.statement: Name every use case as Verb + Noun from the actor's perspective. Reject names that do not pass this test.
rule.rationale: Verb + Noun names (Place Order, Generate Report, Manage User Account) encode the actor's goal; feature names (Order System, Report Module) encode the system's structure.

list.item: What tasks does this actor perform with the system?
What information does the actor need from the system?
What does the actor create, update, or delete?
What events trigger the actor's actions?

rule.statement: Cap the UC count per release at approximately 30. UC sets larger than 30 collapse into documents nobody re-reads.
rule.rationale: Useful UC count is bounded by stakeholder review capacity; beyond 30 per release, the diagram becomes an archive rather than a working tool.

section.name: step-3-create-uc-diagram
section.p: The UC diagram visualizes actors and UCs within the system boundary. Relationships (include/extend/generalization) are added only after all UCs are drafted and reviewed.

rule.statement: Run the relationship pass (include/extend/generalization) last, using only Opus or equivalent, only after all UCs are drafted, and require a one-line rationale per edge.
rule.rationale: Relationship inference is the highest-error part of UC modeling; drafters and reviewers both default to incorrect extend directionality. The relationship pass requires global view of the full UC set.

rule.statement: Each UC spec must have all nine elements: name/ID, description, actors, preconditions, main flow (5-9 steps), alternative flows (at least 1), exception flows (at least 1), postconditions, business rules.
rule.rationale: Missing elements — especially exception flows — produce specs that pass review and fail in production. Postconditions must be observable (DB row, message, file, audit log).

rule.statement: Store UCs as markdown with YAML frontmatter containing id, name, primary_actor, status, version, regulation_anchors, and source. Persist under .aidocs/<feature>/use-cases/UC-NNN-<slug>.md.
rule.rationale: Frontmatter feeds ReqIF export, the index script, and CI checks. Source citations prevent hallucinated requirements.

rule.statement: Use an adversarially framed reviewer prompt (different model or adversarial system prompt) — not the same model that drafted the UC — to score each UC against the 9-item quality checklist.
rule.rationale: Drafter and reviewer using the same model and prompt yields self-congratulatory reviews; adversarial framing surfaces gaps the drafter skipped.

list.item: Are all actor goals covered by at least one UC?
Is the main flow correct from the actor's perspective?
Are alternative flows complete?
Are exception flows handled with observable postconditions?

example.title: Place Order UC Example
example.note: UC-101: Place Order. Primary Actor: Customer (human). System: E-Commerce Platform.
Preconditions: Customer is logged in; shopping cart has at least one item.

Main Flow (5 steps): 1. Customer clicks Checkout — system displays order summary. 2. Customer confirms shipping address — system calculates shipping cost. 3. Customer selects payment method — system displays payment form. 4. Customer enters payment details — system validates payment. 5. Customer clicks Place Order — system creates order and sends confirmation email.
Alternative Flow AF-1 (New Shipping Address): At step 2, customer enters a new address — system validates and saves — returns to step 2.
Exception Flow EX-1 (Payment Declined): At step 4, payment validation fails — system displays error message — customer can retry or select different payment method.
Postconditions (success): order created with status Confirmed; inventory reduced; confirmation email sent.

antipattern.description: Feature as use case — naming a UC "Display Screen" or "Show Report".
antipattern.reason: Features describe system behavior, not actor goals. UCs must be named from the actor's perspective: what does the actor achieve?

antipattern.description: UI nouns in system response — "displays popup", "shows modal", "highlights button".
antipattern.reason: UI elements change across design iterations; UCs must use system-behavior verbs (validates, persists, emits, notifies) to remain stable.

antipattern.description: Main flow with more than 9 steps.
antipattern.reason: Oversized main flows bundle multiple actor goals. Split into separate UCs; each UC covers one goal.

antipattern.description: Omitting exception flows — only happy path documented.
antipattern.reason: Exceptions are where systems fail and users abandon. A UC without exception flows cannot drive test coverage or error-handling design.

antipattern.description: Missing secondary actors: e.g., no Payment Gateway actor on a payment UC.
antipattern.reason: External systems that participate in the interaction belong in the diagram; omitting them produces an incomplete system boundary.

antipattern.description: Too abstract: "Use System" as a use case name.
antipattern.reason: UC names must encode a specific actor goal. Abstract names cannot drive test scaffolding or traceability.

list.item: UML UC diagrams **cannot** show sequencing, timing, or data flow. BAs who encode "first A, then B" in arrows mislead developers — use activity / sequence / BPMN.
<<include>> / <<extend>> are misused ~80% of the time in enterprise UCs; <<extend>> directionality (extension points to base UC) is consistently inverted. LLMs amplify the confusion.
"Actor = role, not person" gets broken constantly — senior managers want their job title on the diagram. Without push-back the diagram becomes an org chart.
UC bloat: enterprise programs spawn UC-001..UC-400 docs nobody re-reads. Useful UC count caps near ~30 per release; beyond that, hierarchy collapses.
Step granularity drifts wildly across BAs and LLM calls (3-step vs. 25-step UCs in the same diagram). Without a fixed step-counting rule, estimation and test scaffolding break.
Microservices / mesh architectures shift the system boundary; UCs assume a closed boundary and mislead when "the system" spans 12 services with their own SLAs.
Agile sprints outdate UC specs from the first review. UC-driven BAs must budget for re-baselining, not spec freeze.

template.purpose: Full use case specification template (ID, actors, preconditions, main/alt/exception flows, postconditions, business rules).
UC diagram template listing actors, UCs per actor, and relationships.
Script to emit a PlantUML UC diagram from a use-case-index.json file.
